skill.name: adaptyv
skill.description: Guides protein designers through Adaptyv cloud laboratory workflows for submitting sequences, tracking assays, retrieving results, and optimizing protein variants before wet-lab validation.
skill.summary: Plan, submit, and track Adaptyv protein validation experiments.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The scanner reported many command-execution and blocker findings, but review shows most are Markdown code fences, inline endpoint notation, protein-science terms, or example API documentation rather than executable skill code. Confirmed risks are legitimate but meaningful: the skill asks agents to use bearer-token authentication, call external web services, optionally configure webhooks, and write downloaded experiment data to local files. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated External API Access
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users and agents to send protein sequences and metadata to the Adaptyv cloud API using bearer-token authentication. This is core functionality, but it can expose proprietary sequences, target choices, webhook URLs, and organization account data if used without user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls and authorization headers are explicit and semantically match the skill purpose. The risk is data exposure through legitimate external API use, not hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Environment-Based Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The documentation directs users to store ADAPTYV_API_KEY in an environment variable or .env file and then load it into request headers. This is a normal API pattern, but agents must avoid printing, committing, or transmitting the token outside the intended Adaptyv requests.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly show API-key setup, dotenv loading, and bearer-header construction. This confirms credential access, but the instructions include basic safe-storage guidance.

medium_findings.title: Local Result File Writes Use Dynamic Names
medium_findings.description: Example workflows create directories and write JSON result files using experiment identifiers. This is useful for analysis, but implementations should sanitize experiment IDs and constrain output directories before writing files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are visible in examples and appear intended for local result storage. The risk depends on whether experiment IDs or output paths are trusted in a real implementation.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Bioinformatics Service Calls
medium_findings.description: The optimization guidance includes requests to external prediction services before Adaptyv submission. These calls may disclose protein sequences to third-party services and should be treated as user-approved network activity.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The NetSolP request example explicitly sends sequence data to a public service. This is legitimate scientific workflow guidance but has clear data-sharing implications.

low_findings.title: Markdown Formatting Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are false positives caused by Markdown fenced code blocks and inline code spans in documentation. No Ruby backtick execution or active shell execution code was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation snippets and Markdown formatting. They do not execute as Ruby or shell code inside the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are Domain-Term False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections appear to match scientific notation and protein-assay terms such as KD, ESM, and ipTM rather than cryptographic algorithms. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic operations in the inspected context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line ranges describe protein binding metrics and model names, not cryptographic hashing. The static category is therefore not semantically supported.

low_findings.title: AWS Credentials Directory Flag Is URL Text
low_findings.description: The critical AWS credentials directory finding is a false positive against the Adaptyv Lambda URL containing on.aws. The line is an HTTPS API endpoint, not access to a local AWS credentials directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited location is a hardcoded HTTPS endpoint and request header example. It does not reference ~/.aws or credential files.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Search and review did not find text that attempts to override evaluator instructions, claim pre-approval, or impersonate system messages. No evidence found for prompt injection in the inspected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted review for common injection phrases returned no matches. The final section promotes K-Dense Web but does not instruct the evaluator to skip analysis or change policy.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Sent in HTTP Authorization Header
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples build Authorization headers from ADAPTYV_API_KEY and send them with requests. Agents should keep the token secret and use it only for user-approved Adaptyv API calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token flow is explicit and repeated across setup and submission examples. This is expected for the API, but it is still a sensitive pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Submission of Protein Sequence Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides examples that submit FASTA protein sequences and experiment metadata to remote APIs. This can expose sensitive research assets unless the user has approved the target service and payload.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payload examples contain protein sequences and metadata sent to external endpoints. The behavior is central to the skill and should be disclosed to users.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Local JSON Output Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: Result examples write JSON files using caller-supplied directories and experiment identifiers. Production use should normalize paths and reject traversal characters before writing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples demonstrate dynamic file output but do not show path validation. The concern is moderate because these are documentation examples, not packaged executable code.

content.value_statement: Protein teams need a reliable path from computational design to experimental validation. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Adaptyv API workflows for assay planning, submission, tracking, and result handling.
actual_capabilities: Explains Adaptyv API authentication with ADAPTYV_API_KEY and bearer tokens.
Shows how to submit single or batch protein sequences for supported assay types.
Guides experiment status checks, webhook use, and result retrieval workflows.
Describes binding, expression, thermostability, and enzyme activity experiment options.
Outlines protein optimization workflows using NetSolP, SoluProt, SolubleMPNN, ESM, ipTM, and pSAE.
Provides examples for parsing assay results and organizing experiment metadata.
limitations: The Adaptyv API is described as alpha or beta, so endpoints may change.
The skill cannot guarantee laboratory results, assay turnaround, or target availability.
Users must provide valid Adaptyv credentials and approve any external sequence submission.
Example code needs security review before production use, especially path handling and webhook validation.

use_cases.title: Screen Protein Variants
use_cases.description: Plan a batch of variants, select an assay type, and prepare sequences for Adaptyv submission.

use_cases.title: Track Cloud Lab Experiments
use_cases.description: Check experiment status, monitor credits, and organize result retrieval for active validation campaigns.

use_cases.title: Improve Expression Before Testing
use_cases.description: Use solubility and sequence-likelihood guidance to rank variants before wet-lab validation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an Assay
prompt_templates.prompt: I have protein sequences and want to validate them with Adaptyv. Help me choose between binding, expression, thermostability, and enzyme activity assays.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Submission
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Adaptyv submission plan for these FASTA sequences. Include required metadata, target information, and checks before any API call.

prompt_templates.title: Track and Retrieve Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me check Adaptyv experiment status, decide whether to use webhooks or polling, and organize completed results for analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize and Validate a Library
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a protein optimization workflow using solubility, ESM, and structure-based checks, then prepare the top variants for Adaptyv testing.

output_examples.input: A researcher asks which assay to run for antibody candidates against a known antigen.
output_examples.output: A concise assay recommendation that compares binding and expression testing, lists required target details, and identifies controls to include.

output_examples.input: A team has an experiment ID and needs to handle results safely.
output_examples.output: A retrieval checklist covering authentication, status confirmation, local output naming, result parsing, and sensitive data handling.

output_examples.input: A computational biologist wants to submit only the best variants from a large library.
output_examples.output: A staged optimization plan that screens solubility, ranks sequence likelihood, checks structural concerns, and selects variants for wet-lab validation.

best_practices: Confirm user approval before sending sequences, targets, metadata, or webhook URLs to external services.
Keep ADAPTYV_API_KEY in environment variables or secret storage and never include it in logs or shared files.
Validate FASTA format, assay type, target identifiers, and output paths before running API workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not submit proprietary sequences to Adaptyv or third-party prediction services without explicit approval.
Do not paste API keys into prompts, notebooks, reports, or committed .env files.
Do not use unsanitized experiment IDs or user-provided directories as file paths.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan Adaptyv protein validation experiments, use the API, track status, retrieve results, and optimize sequences before testing.

faq.question: Does it run lab experiments by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and API examples. Actual experiments require Adaptyv access, credentials, credits, and user-approved submission.

faq.question: Which assay types are covered?
faq.answer: The documentation covers binding assays, expression testing, thermostability testing, and enzyme activity assays.

faq.question: Can it help optimize protein sequences?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes workflows using solubility prediction, sequence redesign, language-model scoring, and structure-based checks.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: The examples use an ADAPTYV_API_KEY stored in an environment variable or local .env file.

faq.question: What security precautions matter most?
faq.answer: Protect API keys, get approval before external submissions, avoid logging sensitive payloads, and sanitize output paths before writing files.
